content.text: Follow the instructions at
[@play.md](file:///Users/softmax/bot/play.md)

<context ref="file:///Users/softmax/bot/play.md">
# Participate in Crewrift Daily

You are a coding agent helping a human build, test, submit, and improve a Coworld player for the
Crewrift Daily Softmax league. Keep the human in the loop: before changing the player, summarize what
the evidence shows, propose one focused change, and ask before making it. Never submit to a public league until
the human explicitly asks you to.

## This league

- League: `league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f` (Crewrift Daily)
- League page: https://softmax.com/observatory/v2#tab=leagues&detail=league:league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f
- Coworld: `cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b` (`crewrift`)

Visible divisions:

- `div_71f55782-07fc-4375-a5c0-d0b3a5c21c6d`: Qualifiers (level -99, type `staging`)
- `div_8d3ead22-1244-49f5-8ee8-1bd150be2f6e`: Daily (level 1, type `competition`)

## Docs

There are two sources of truth. Read them in order and follow their links instead of hunting elsewhere:

1. The [Coworld package README](https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld/blob/main/README.md) — how the `coworld` CLI works and the map to its
   cookbook and player docs (download a Coworld, run local episodes, build, upload, and submit a player). Run
   `uv run coworld --help` for the command surface.
2. [Crewrift Daily README](https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld-crewrift/blob/master/README.md) — the game-specific source of truth for rules, scoring, starter players, recommended
   local variants, Dockerfile and run command, and the player and global protocols. Start here for anything
   game-specific; it links onward to whatever else you need.

After you download the Coworld, the player and global protocols are also available directly in the manifest
under `game.protocols.player` and `game.protocols.global`. Read the player protocol before writing player code.

## Working agreement

- Start by asking whether the human wants to kick the tires, land a first valid submission, or push for
  leaderboard strength. Submissions can be revised, so it is fine to start small and iterate.
- Ask about constraints before choosing a strategy: deterministic code, LLM usage, optimization work, time
  budget, and whether a public submission is acceptable.
- Before editing the player, show the relevant replay/log evidence, propose one change, and ask for approval.
- After each league result, explain what happened in plain language before proposing the next iteration.
- When docs, commands, runtime behavior, logs, or replays disagree, preserve the evidence and file an issue in
  the Coworld repo: https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld/issues. Include the command, league/Coworld ids,
  links to logs or replays, and the smallest repro.

Keep a `README.md` (objective, target league, Coworld id/ref, Crewrift Daily README link, run commands, image tag,
replay/log locations, current strategy) and an `AGENTS.md` (pointing future agents at the docs above and this
working agreement) in your project as you go.

## (a) Set up

Confirm Docker and `uv`, create a project, and add the CLI. If `docker info` works, use that existing Docker provider;
otherwise start or install Colima, Podman, OrbStack, Docker Desktop, or Docker Engine.

```bash
docker --version && docker info && uv --version
mkdir coworld-crewrift-player && cd coworld-crewrift-player
uv init --bare --name coworld-crewrift-player
uv add "coworld[auth]"
```

## (b) Inspect and interview

Download the Coworld and prove the baseline runs before writing any player code:

```bash
uv run coworld download cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b
uv run coworld run-episode ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json --timeout-seconds 120
```

Read the Crewrift Daily README, choose a starter/baseline player, and write the goal, constraints, and first plan in
`README.md` before implementing.

## (c) Build your player

A player is a Docker image that connects to `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL`, speaks the player protocol (from the
Crewrift Daily README and the manifest's `game.protocols.player`), plays to the end of the episode, and exits cleanly.
Start from the starter/baseline players in the downloaded manifest under `player[]`: open that player's `source_url`,
keep its original Dockerfile context, and adapt it. Do not copy only a player subdirectory unless its Dockerfile
explicitly supports that.

For a GitHub starter source like `https://github.com/ORG/REPO/tree/BRANCH/path/to/player`, build with the repo root as
context:

```bash
git clone --depth 1 --branch BRANCH https://github.com/ORG/REPO.git source/repo
docker build --platform=linux/amd64 -f source/repo/path/to/player/Dockerfile -t my-player:latest source/repo
uv run coworld run-episode ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json my-player:latest --timeout-seconds 120
```

Watch the replay and logs, tell the human what happened, propose one change, and iterate. Use the Crewrift Daily README for
game-specific build or `--run` details.

## (d) Upload and submit

When the human asks you to submit, authenticate and upload:

```bash
uv run softmax login   # use `softmax login --no-browser` in a headless or remote agent
uv run coworld upload-policy my-player:latest --name "$USER-my-player"
uv run coworld submit "$USER-my-player" --league league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f
```

Then watch results from the league page or `uv run coworld results league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f`. Report the status, outcome,
logs/replays, clearest weakness, and one proposed next change.

</context>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

I've read the Crewrift Daily play instructions. Before diving in, a few questions:

**What's your goal right now?**
1. **Kick the tires** — just get the environment set up and see a local episode run
2. **Land a first valid submission** — get something working end-to-end in the league
3. **Push for leaderboard strength** — optimize an existing player for better results

**Constraints to know about:**
- Are you okay with LLM-powered players, or prefer deterministic code only?
- Any time budget? (e.g., "I have 30 minutes" vs "let's go deep")
- Is submitting to the public league okay, or do you want to stay in qualifiers first?

Let me know and I'll get started with setup.

- 1. For now, kick the tires.
- I'm not pro or against LLM-powered players, let's just use what's best to win.
- My time budget is around 4hs.
- We won't submit to the league until we have something that more or less works. Wait for my command on that one.

file.content:
# AGENTS.md

Guidance for coding agents working from this downloaded Coworld package.

## Facts

- Champion means your nominated policy version: the chosen participant you want to represent you in a league. It is not
  a claim that the policy has won the tournament.

## Start

- Read `coworld_manifest.json` before changing policy code.
- Treat `game.protocols.player`, `game.docs.pages`, `variants`, and `certification` as the local contract for this
  package.
- Run `uv run coworld run-episode ./coworld_manifest.json --timeout-seconds 120` with the bundled players before
  testing your own image.

## Policy Work

- Keep policy source in your policy project, not in this downloaded Coworld cache.
- Use the manifest path from this directory when building, running, and comparing policies.

content.content:
# Sprite v1 Protocol Specification

## WebSocket Connection
The protocol operates over WebSocket at a full URL endpoint (e.g., `ws://localhost:8080/global`). Connection details specify complete WebSocket URLs rather than relative paths.

## Message Structure
Every message begins with a single byte indicating type, followed by little-endian encoded fields. Variable-length payloads include length prefixes before data bytes.

## Server-to-Client Messages

**Define Sprite (0x01)**
- Sprite id (u16), Width (u16), Height (u16), Compressed length (u32)
- Compressed pixels as Snappy stream decompressing to "Width * Height * 4 bytes"
- Label length (u16) and UTF-8 label bytes
- Pixels use unpremultiplied RGBA (0-255 range)

**Define Object (0x02)**
- Object id, X position (i16), Y position (i16), Z order (i16), Layer (u8), Sprite id (u16)

**Delete Object (0x03)**
- Object id only

**Clear Objects (0x04)**
- No additional fields; sprite definitions persist

**Set Viewport (0x05)**
- Layer id, Width (u16), Height (u16)

**Define Layer (0x06)**
- Layer id, Type (0x00-0x08), Flags

## Client-to-Server Messages

**Input Text (0x81)**
- Length (u16), ASCII bytes array
- Printable ASCII (0x20-0x7e) represents characters; control codes: 0x08 (backspace), 0x09 (tab), 0x0a (enter), 0x1b (escape)

**Mouse Position (0x82)**
- X, Y (i16), Layer id

**Mouse Button (0x83)**
- Code (u8), Down flag (0 or 1)
- Suggested: 0x01 (left), 0x02 (right), 0x03 (middle)

**Player Input (0x84)**
- Button bitmask: bits represent D-pad (0-3), Select (4), A (5), B (6)

## Rendering Rules
Objects render by layer, then by Z-order ascending, then Y-coordinate ascending, then object ID ascending. Clipping occurs at layer viewport boundaries.

content.content:
# Crewrift: AI Social Deductions Game - Summary

## Game Overview
Crewrift is a multiplayer social deduction Coworld game designed for AI benchmarking. Most players are crewmates who complete tasks and vote out imposters, while a few imposters blend in, eliminate crew members, and survive votes.

## Core Rules

**Crewmate Objectives:**
- Complete assigned tasks by navigating to locations and holding the action button
- Vote out imposters during emergency meetings
- Win by completing all tasks or eliminating all imposters

**Imposter Mechanics:**
- Kill crewmates with cooldown-limited abilities
- Access vents for rapid map traversal
- Win by reducing crew to imposter parity
- Can "self-report" bodies to create confusion

**Voting System:**
Anyone can trigger votes by pressing the emergency button (once per game) or reporting bodies. Players discuss and vote; votes are final and cannot be changed.

## Scoring System
- Winning: +100 points
- Task completion: +1 point each
- Killing crewmates: +10 points each
- Failing to vote: -10 points
- Idleness with pending tasks: -1 point per 10 seconds

## Running Locally

**Requirements:** Nim 2.2.10 (using Nimby for version management)

**Local Build:**
```
COGAME_HOST=0.0.0.0 COGAME_PORT=2000 COGAME_CONFIG_URI=file://$PWD/config.json nim r src/crewrift.nim
```

**Running 8 Bots:**
Connect compiled bots via WebSocket URLs following the pattern `ws://localhost:2000/player?slot=N&token=0xBADA55_N`

## Docker Deployment

The repository provides public Softmax images requiring no compilation:

```
docker network create crewrift-local
docker run --rm -d --name crewrift-server --network crewrift-local -p 2000:2000 -v "$PWD/config.json:/workspace/crewrift/config.json:ro" -e COGAME_HOST=0.0.0.0 -e COGAME_PORT=2000 -e COGAME_CONFIG_URI=file:///workspace/crewrift/config.json public.ecr.aws/s3j4p9s7/treeform/games/crewrift:latest
```

## Player Development

Players communicate via the **Bitworld Sprite v1 protocol** and receive a `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL` environment variable.

**Starting Points:**
- Use the stock `notsus` baseline for comparison
- Modify `players/notsus/notsus.nim` for incremental improvements
- Implement Sprite v1 from scratch in any language as a Docker image

## Replay Analysis

The `tools/expand_replay.nim` tool converts replay files to readable timelines showing ticks, phase changes, movement, task events, kills, votes, and scoring—enabling rapid iteration on bot improvements.

## Resources

- Protocol details: Bitworld Sprite v1 documentation
- Coworld CLI guide: softmax.com/play_crewrift.md
- Issue reporting: GitHub issues repository (distinguish between Crewrift and Coworld CLI problems)

toolUseResult.result:
# Crewrift: AI Social Deductions Game - Summary

## Game Overview
Crewrift is a multiplayer social deduction Coworld game designed for AI benchmarking. Most players are crewmates who complete tasks and vote out imposters, while a few imposters blend in, eliminate crew members, and survive votes.

## Core Rules

**Crewmate Objectives:**
- Complete assigned tasks by navigating to locations and holding the action button
- Vote out imposters during emergency meetings
- Win by completing all tasks or eliminating all imposters

**Imposter Mechanics:**
- Kill crewmates with cooldown-limited abilities
- Access vents for rapid map traversal
- Win by reducing crew to imposter parity
- Can "self-report" bodies to create confusion

**Voting System:**
Anyone can trigger votes by pressing the emergency button (once per game) or reporting bodies. Players discuss and vote; votes are final and cannot be changed.

## Scoring System
- Winning: +100 points
- Task completion: +1 point each
- Killing crewmates: +10 points each
- Failing to vote: -10 points
- Idleness with pending tasks: -1 point per 10 seconds

## Running Locally

**Requirements:** Nim 2.2.10 (using Nimby for version management)

**Local Build:**
```
COGAME_HOST=0.0.0.0 COGAME_PORT=2000 COGAME_CONFIG_URI=file://$PWD/config.json nim r src/crewrift.nim
```

**Running 8 Bots:**
Connect compiled bots via WebSocket URLs following the pattern `ws://localhost:2000/player?slot=N&token=0xBADA55_N`

## Docker Deployment

The repository provides public Softmax images requiring no compilation:

```
docker network create crewrift-local
docker run --rm -d --name crewrift-server --network crewrift-local -p 2000:2000 -v "$PWD/config.json:/workspace/crewrift/config.json:ro" -e COGAME_HOST=0.0.0.0 -e COGAME_PORT=2000 -e COGAME_CONFIG_URI=file:///workspace/crewrift/config.json public.ecr.aws/s3j4p9s7/treeform/games/crewrift:latest
```

## Player Development

Players communicate via the **Bitworld Sprite v1 protocol** and receive a `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL` environment variable.

**Starting Points:**
- Use the stock `notsus` baseline for comparison
- Modify `players/notsus/notsus.nim` for incremental improvements
- Implement Sprite v1 from scratch in any language as a Docker image

## Replay Analysis

The `tools/expand_replay.nim` tool converts replay files to readable timelines showing ticks, phase changes, movement, task events, kills, votes, and scoring—enabling rapid iteration on bot improvements.

## Resources

- Protocol details: Bitworld Sprite v1 documentation
- Coworld CLI guide: softmax.com/play_crewrift.md
- Issue reporting: GitHub issues repository (distinguish between Crewrift and Coworld CLI problems)

content.content:
# Crewrift Repository Structure

Based on the GitHub page, here are the top-level directories and files visible in the repo:

## Directories
- `.github/workflows` - CI/CD workflows
- `data` - Game data files
- `docs` - Documentation
- `players/notsus` - Example bot implementation
- `reporters/eventlog` - Event logging functionality
- `src` - Main game source code
- `tests` - Test files
- `tools` - Utility tools (includes `expand_replay.nim`)

## Key Files
- `README.md` - Main documentation
- `config.json` - Game configuration
- `coworld_manifest.json` - Coworld manifest
- `crewrift.nimble` - Nim package file
- `Dockerfile` - Container definition
- `LICENSE` - MIT license
- `nimby.lock` - Dependency lock file

## Starter Code
The primary example is **`players/notsus/`**, which contains a baseline bot implementation. The documentation states: "edit `players/notsus/notsus.nim` and use `players/notsus/README.md` as the source guide" for improving the baseline or developing custom policies from scratch.

Extract the complete source code of this file. Include every line, function, import, and comment. This is the player bot source code I need to understand.

content.content:
# Summary of Notsus Bot README

## Overview
Notsus is a basic example bot for Crewrift that demonstrates fundamental gameplay mechanics. The documentation notes that "it is not the ultimate bot. It's very stupid," but serves as a learning foundation for developing improved strategies.

## Running the Bot

**Headless mode:**
Set the `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL` environment variable and execute: `nim r players/notsus/notsus.nim`

**With visual debugger:**
Add the `-d:notsusGui` flag to display sprite viewports, walkability masks, player positions, paths, and velocity information.

## Core Functionality Areas

**Protocol & Communication:**
- Parses sprite protocol and sends key/text packets
- Handles screen transitions: joining → role reveal → gameplay → voting → results

**Navigation:**
- Uses A* pathfinding with the decompressed walkability map
- Implements PID controller for momentum-based movement
- Tracks absolute map coordinates for all sprites

**Crewmate Strategy:**
- Completes tasks efficiently, prioritizing closest objectives
- Monitors other players' activities
- Leverages radar information to locate tasks

**Imposter Strategy:**
- Fakes task completion and isolates victims
- Kills without witnesses when possible
- Uses strategic body reporting and self-reporting tactics
- Employs randomness to avoid detection patterns

**Voting Phase:**
- Parses voting screens using directional pad navigation
- Reports body locations using room information
- Clears allies while venting suspicion toward actual impostors

## Technical Architecture

The codebase divides into protocol handling (`notsus/protocols.nim`) and strategy logic (`notsus.nim`). Key functions handle frame loops, sprite packet parsing, role detection, pathfinding, and voting decisions.

The documentation recommends studying replays to identify bot failures and iteratively improving specific capabilities.

content.content:
# Summary of notsus.nim Bot Source Code

This is a sophisticated bot implementation for the "Among Us"-style game Crewrift, written in Nim. Here's what I can tell you about its structure:

## Core Components

**File Purpose**: The bot player implementation that navigates the game world, completes tasks, identifies imposters, and participates in voting.

**Key Sections**:

1. **Imports & Constants** (lines 1-330): Defines dozens of configuration parameters like screen dimensions, patch search sizes, color palettes, camera constraints, and task-related thresholds.

2. **Type Definitions** (lines ~330-550): Establishes data structures for:
   - Tile knowledge (unknown/open/wall states)
   - Bot role tracking (crewmate vs. imposter)
   - Path nodes and steps for navigation
   - Camera scoring and patch matching
   - Vote slot information
   - Visible entities (crewmates, bodies, ghosts, tasks)

3. **Utility Functions** (lines ~550-800): Helper procedures for:
   - Map coordinate conversions
   - Camera positioning calculations
   - Room/task identification
   - Sprite color sampling

4. **Core Algorithm Functions**: Procedures for:
   - Camera localization using patch hashing
   - Frame pixel analysis
   - Sprite detection and matching

## Notable Features

The bot uses "patch-based localization"—dividing the screen into 8×8 patches and comparing their hashes against known map regions to determine camera position. It tracks visible entities, manages task completion, handles voting logic, and includes performance profiling with microsecond-level measurements.

The code is highly optimized with constants for search radii, thresholds, and timing parameters throughout gameplay.

content.content:
# Dockerfile Content

Here is the complete Dockerfile from the `coworld-crewrift/players/notsus/` directory:

```dockerfile
# Build Docker.
FROM debian:bookworm-slim AS build

RUN apt-get update && \
apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
build-essential \
ca-certificates \
curl \
git && \
rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN if [ "$(dpkg --print-architecture)" = "amd64" ]; then \
curl -fsSL \
-o /usr/local/bin/nimby \
https://github.com/treeform/nimby/releases/download/0.1.26/nimby-Linux-X64; \
elif [ "$(dpkg --print-architecture)" = "arm64" ]; then \
curl -fsSL \
-o /usr/local/bin/nimby \
https://github.com/treeform/nimby/releases/download/0.1.26/nimby-Linux-ARM64; \
else \
echo "unsupported arch: $(dpkg --print-architecture)" && exit 1; \
fi && \
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/nimby && \
nimby use 2.2.4

ENV PATH="/root/.nimby/nim/bin:$PATH"

WORKDIR /workspace/crewrift

COPY nimby.lock .

RUN nimby --global sync nimby.lock

COPY . .

RUN nim c \
-d:release \
-d:botHeadless \
-d:useMalloc \
--opt:speed \
--stackTrace:on \
--nimcache:/tmp/crewrift-nimcache \
--out:notsus \
players/notsus/notsus.nim

# Run Docker.
FROM debian:bookworm-slim

RUN apt-get update && \
apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
ca-certificates \
libcurl4 && \
rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /workspace/crewrift

COPY --from=build /workspace/crewrift/notsus /bin/notsus

CMD ["/bin/notsus"]
```

This multi-stage build compiles a Nim application and packages it into a minimal runtime container.

content.text: Now I have a solid understanding. The `notsus` player is built in Nim and requires the full repo as build context (it uses root-level `nimby.lock` and compiles from the repo root). Let me look at what makes a simpler approach — we could write our player in Python which would be faster to iterate on. Let me check the protocol more carefully.

input.prompt: Extract the complete source code of this protocols.nim file. Include every line. I need to understand the WebSocket protocol implementation - message types, binary formats, how to send inputs, how to receive game state.

content.content:
# protocols.nim WebSocket Implementation Analysis

Based on the GitHub repository content provided, here's the complete protocols.nim source code structure and key implementation details:

## File Overview
The file contains **525 lines** implementing sprite protocol handling for WebSocket communication in the coworld-crewrift project.

## Key Components

### Data Structures
- **SpriteInfo**: Stores sprite metadata (dimensions, label, pixel data)
- **ObjectState**: Tracks object positioning (x, y, z coordinates, layer, sprite reference)
- **ProtocolClient**: Main connection state managing sprites, objects, camera, and walkability data

### Message Handling

**Input Methods:**
- `inputBlob(mask: uint8)` - "Builds one sprite player input packet"
- `chatBlob(text: string)` - "Builds one sprite player chat packet"

**Reception:**
The `receiveLatestFrameInto` procedure handles WebSocket message reception:
- Receives binary sprite protocol packets
- Responds to ping messages with pong
- Drains up to 128 queued frames
- Tracks frame advancement and drops

### Binary Frame Format

**4-bit Packing:**
- `unpack4bpp()` expands packed 4-bit framebuffer into palette indices
- `pack4bpp()` compresses palette indices into 4-bit layout
- Frame dimensions: 128x128 pixels

### Rendering Pipeline

The implementation includes object rendering with painter's algorithm ordering by z-depth, then y-coordinate, preserving layer 0 visibility.

**Game State Reception:** Binary sprite protocol messages convey sprite definitions, object positions, and scene updates through the WebSocket connection.

# protocols.nim WebSocket Implementation Analysis

Based on the GitHub repository content provided, here's the complete protocols.nim source code structure and key implementation details:

## File Overview
The file contains **525 lines** implementing sprite protocol handling for WebSocket communication in the coworld-crewrift project.

## Key Components

### Data Structures
- **SpriteInfo**: Stores sprite metadata (dimensions, label, pixel data)
- **ObjectState**: Tracks object positioning (x, y, z coordinates, layer, sprite reference)
- **ProtocolClient**: Main connection state managing sprites, objects, camera, and walkability data

### Message Handling

**Input Methods:**
- `inputBlob(mask: uint8)` - "Builds one sprite player input packet"
- `chatBlob(text: string)` - "Builds one sprite player chat packet"

**Reception:**
The `receiveLatestFrameInto` procedure handles WebSocket message reception:
- Receives binary sprite protocol packets
- Responds to ping messages with pong
- Drains up to 128 queued frames
- Tracks frame advancement and drops

### Binary Frame Format

**4-bit Packing:**
- `unpack4bpp()` expands packed 4-bit framebuffer into palette indices
- `pack4bpp()` compresses palette indices into 4-bit layout
- Frame dimensions: 128x128 pixels

### Rendering Pipeline

The implementation includes object rendering with painter's algorithm ordering by z-depth, then y-coordinate, preserving layer 0 visibility.

**Game State Reception:** Binary sprite protocol messages convey sprite definitions, object positions, and scene updates through the WebSocket connection.

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. The protocol is:
1. Connect via WebSocket to `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL`
2. Receive binary sprite protocol messages (define sprites, objects, viewport, etc.)
3. Send `PlayerInput` (0x84) with button bitmask for movement/actions
4. Send `InputText` (0x81) for chat during meetings

The player renders a virtual screen and uses computer-vision techniques to figure out where it is and what's happening. This is a pixel-based protocol — players see rendered sprites and must interpret visual information.

Let me clone the repo to get the notsus source and build it.

Let me look at the results that were produced before timeout, and also try with a longer timeout or the `--variant default` to control the episode length.

content.text:
The notsus bot connects and then loses the connection. This is the certification fixture with maxTicks=300, which is very short — the game ends quickly via time limit and all bots just draw with 0 scores. The bots are connecting successfully but there's just not enough ticks for them to do anything meaningful (300 ticks with the amd64 emulation overhead).

Let me check if there's a way to use the "default" variant which has 10000 ticks, and try with a longer timeout.

uv run coworld play --help 2>&1 | grep -A3 variant

content.text:
The bot connects and the game starts (the "Dropped message" is gone now), plays 300 ticks, and draws. The notsus bot gets a "connection lost" because the game server ends the connection after the episode finishes. The problem is: 300 ticks at emulated speed simply isn't enough time for the bots to do anything meaningful before the time limit hits.

Let me try passing the episode request JSON directly (this is what the `run-episode` positional argument `episode_request_or_player_images` is for).

content.text:
Excellent! Now we have a real game. Here's what happened:

**Episode Summary:**
- 8 players, 2 imposters (Player5=pink, Player8=pale blue)
- Crew won by completing all 8 tasks each AND voting out an imposter
- The bots used a "reset imposter cooldowns" strategy — calling emergency meetings early to prevent kills
- After 6 meetings, pink (imposter) finally killed red, but then pale blue reported the body
- During the vote, 4 crew members correctly identified "pink sus" and voted pink out
- Crew won! Scores: 108 for each crew (100 win + 8 tasks), 10 for pink (1 kill), 0 for pale blue

The `notsus` bot is quite competent: it does tasks, communicates evidence during meetings, and votes correctly. The pipeline is working end-to-end.

Now let me set up the project README and prepare to write our own player. Let me first understand what we're working with for the actual league submission.

The notsus bot works well — it tracks who's alive/dead, reads chat messages, identifies "sus" players, and votes accordingly. The "connection lost" at the end is just the game server closing after crew wins.

Now let me write the README and AGENTS.md, then summarize where we stand.

input.content:
# Crewrift Daily Player

## Objective

Build a competitive player for the Crewrift Daily league — an Among Us-style social deduction game
with 8 players (6 crew, 2 imposters), tasks, kills, and voting.

## Target League

- League: `league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f` (Crewrift Daily)
- Coworld: `cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b` (crewrift 0.1.38)
- Crewrift Daily README: https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld-crewrift/blob/master/README.md

## Scoring

- Win: +100 points
- Task completion: +1 per task (8 tasks per player)
- Kill crewmate: +10 per kill
- Fail to vote: -10
- Idle with pending tasks: -1 per 10s

## Run Commands

```bash
# Run a full local episode with notsus baseline (default variant, 10000 ticks)
uv run coworld play ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json notsus:latest --run "/bin/notsus" --variant default --timeout-seconds 600 --no-open-browser -o ./results_play1

# Run certification episode (short, 300 ticks)
uv run coworld run-episode ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json notsus:latest --run "/bin/notsus" --timeout-seconds 300

# Build the notsus baseline
docker build --platform=linux/amd64 -f source/coworld-crewrift/players/notsus/Dockerfile -t notsus:latest source/coworld-crewrift

# Submit (when ready)
uv run coworld upload-policy my-player:latest --name "softmax-my-player"
uv run coworld submit "softmax-my-player" --league league_605ff338-0a2e-4e62-aeda-559df9a9198f
```

## Replay / Log Locations

- Results: `results_play1/results.json`
- Logs: `results_play1/logs/`
- Replay: `results_play1/replay/`

## Current Strategy

Using the `notsus` baseline player (Nim, Sprite v1 protocol, patch-based localization, A* pathfinding, evidence-based voting). Investigating how to improve or build a custom player.

## Protocol

Players connect via WebSocket (env var `COWORLD_PLAYER_WS_URL`), receive binary Sprite v1 frames
(128x128 4bpp rendered game view), and send:
- `PlayerInput` (0x84): D-pad + buttons for movement/actions
- `InputText` (0x81): Chat messages during voting

The game is pixel-based — players must interpret rendered sprites to navigate, find tasks, detect kills, and participate in meetings.

# AGENTS.md

## Quick Start

1. Read `play.md` in the project root for the working agreement and setup steps.
2. Read the [Crewrift Daily README](https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld-crewrift/blob/master/README.md) for game rules.
3. Read the [Sprite v1 protocol](https://github.com/Metta-AI/bitworld/blob/master/docs/sprite_v1.md) for the player↔game interface.
4. The Coworld manifest is at `coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json`.

## Key Facts

- The `notsus` baseline source is in `source/coworld-crewrift/players/notsus/`.
- Building notsus requires the full repo as Docker context (uses root `nimby.lock`).
- The `play` command with `--variant default` gives 10000-tick games; `run-episode` uses the short 300-tick certification fixture.
- Platform mismatch warning (amd64 on arm64) is normal — emulation works but is slower.
- Players must be Docker images targeting `linux/amd64`.

## Working Agreement

- Before changing the player, show evidence, propose one change, ask for approval.
- Never submit to the public league without explicit human permission.
- Keep README.md updated with current strategy and results.

content.text:
Here's where we stand:

**Setup complete. Tires kicked successfully.**

What we proved:
- Docker, `uv`, and the `coworld` CLI all work
- Downloaded the Crewrift Coworld (v0.1.38)
- Built the `notsus` baseline player from source
- Ran a full 10000-tick episode — **crew won** with scores of 108 (100 win + 8 tasks each)
- The `notsus` bot does real gameplay: A* pathfinding, task completion, evidence-based voting, chat communication

**Key observations from the game:**
- The notsus bots call emergency meetings early ("just resetting imposter cool downs") — clever anti-imposter tactic
- When a body was found, bots correctly identified the killer ("body in bridge sus pink") and voted them out
- Imposters scored poorly (10 and 0) — the current baseline heavily favors crew

**Next steps when you're ready:**
1. We could start writing our own player (Python would iterate faster than Nim)
2. Or we could fork/modify notsus directly to improve imposter play
3. Or analyze more episodes to find weaknesses to exploit

What direction appeals to you?
is notsus ours?

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether `notsus` is "ours" — meaning, can we use it as our submission, or is it the public baseline that everyone else also has access to?

Looking at what I know:
- `notsus` is the starter/baseline player included in the public `coworld-crewrift` repo
- The manifest lists `crewriftstarter` as the bundled player (which is from the private `Metta-AI/players` repo)
- `notsus` is in the public game repo at `players/notsus/`

So no — `notsus` is the public baseline that everyone in the league has access to. 

That means we'd essentially be submitting the starter code, which won't give us any competitive advantage. We'd need to build something better or at least modify it significantly to stand out.

content.text:
No. `notsus` is the public baseline that ships with the [Crewrift game repo](https://github.com/Metta-AI/coworld-crewrift) — everyone in the league has access to it. Submitting it unmodified would be like submitting the starter code in a competition.

The manifest also bundles `crewriftstarter` (from a private `Metta-AI/players` repo) which adds a Bedrock/Anthropic LLM for meeting chat — that's also not ours.

We need to build our own player. Options:

1. **Fork notsus** (Nim) — modify strategy directly, benefit from its working pathfinding/vision/voting code. Downside: Nim is less convenient to iterate on.
2. **Write from scratch in Python** — faster iteration, easier to integrate an LLM for social deduction. Downside: we'd need to reimplement the Sprite v1 protocol and pathfinding.
3. **Hybrid** — keep notsus for navigation/mechanics, add an LLM layer for smarter meeting behavior (the biggest leverage point).

Given the scoring and what we saw, the biggest weakness to exploit is **imposter play** (the baseline imposters scored 0–10 while crew scored 108). A player that's a strong imposter would dominate. Want me to dig deeper into a specific direction?
ok how can I run and watch a game?

content.thinking: The user wants to watch a game live. The `coworld play` command has a `--open-browser` flag (which is the default) and showed "Global client" and "Player clients" URLs in the output. Let me show them how to run a game and watch it in the browser.

content.text:
Use the `play` command — it opens a browser viewer by default:

```bash
cd /Users/softmax/bot/coworld-crewrift-player
uv run coworld play ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json notsus:latest --run "/bin/notsus" --variant default --timeout-seconds 600
```

When it starts, it prints URLs like:

- **Global client** — spectator view of the whole map
- **Player clients** (0–7) — what each individual bot sees

It should auto-open the global view in your browser. If not, just click the "Global client" URL from the output.

Want me to kick one off now?
sure

content.text:
Same pattern as before (same seed produces nearly identical games). Crew wins again 108-10-0. Did the viewer open for you? You can also replay it after the fact:

```bash
uv run coworld replay ./coworld/cow_b805fb6b-ded6-46ac-a276-41fb831f2c7b/coworld_manifest.json /Users/softmax/bot/coworld-crewrift-player/tmp/coworld-play-f6_bqpfx/replay
```

Want me to run another game with a different seed so you can see varied gameplay? Or are you ready to talk strategy for building our player?

The viewer worked perfectly.
Now tell me a little bit more about the bot that we can create.
It should handle crew and imposter scenarios right?

content.thinking: The user wants to understand what our custom bot needs to do. Let me explain the dual-role nature of the game and what capabilities the bot needs.

content.text:
Yes, exactly. Every player gets randomly assigned either crew or imposter at the start of each game, so our bot needs to handle both roles well.

**As Crew (75% of the time — 6 out of 8 slots):**
- Navigate the map and complete tasks (walk to location, hold action button)
- Watch for suspicious behavior (someone following you, not doing tasks, near bodies)
- Report dead bodies when found
- Communicate evidence during meetings ("body in reactor, saw pink nearby")
- Vote correctly based on evidence

**As Imposter (25% of the time — 2 out of 8 slots):**
- Fake doing tasks (stand near task locations convincingly)
- Kill crewmates when no witnesses are present
- Use vents for fast travel and escape after kills
- Self-report bodies to deflect suspicion
- Lie convincingly during meetings ("I was in medbay")
- Frame innocent players

**What we saw from notsus:**
- Crew play is solid — tasks get done, evidence is shared, votes land
- Imposter play is terrible — scored 0–10 while crew scored 108. The imposters barely kill anyone and never win

**The big opportunity is imposter play.** If our bot can win even 30–40% of games as imposter (vs notsus's ~0%), we'd dominate the league. The scoring rewards kills (+10 each) and wins (+100), so a single imposter win with 3 kills = 130 points.

**Implementation approach — two main options:**

1. **Vision-based (like notsus):** Parse the 128x128 pixel frames, do pathfinding, detect players/tasks/bodies from sprites. Hard but fully autonomous.

2. **LLM-augmented:** Use the vision layer for navigation/mechanics, but call an LLM for social decisions — who to kill, what to say in meetings, who to frame. This is where the biggest edge is, especially for imposter deception.

The protocol is the same either way — connect via WebSocket, receive pixel frames, send button presses and chat text. The question is how smart we make the decision-making layer.

Want to go the LLM-augmented route for meetings/deception, or keep it purely algorithmic?

What's the best strategy for an imposter?
I was thinking collaboration as much as possible, even helping uncover the other imposter, then betrayal when the crew members lessen.

What do you think?

content.thinking:
The user is asking about imposter strategy. They have an interesting idea about collaborating with crew and even helping uncover the other imposter, then betraying when numbers are low. Let me think about this strategically.

In Among Us-style games, the scoring system is:
- Win: +100 points
- Kill: +10 per kill
- Task completion: +1 per task (imposters don't do real tasks)
- Fail to vote: -10

Key constraints:
- 8 players, 2 imposters, 6 crew
- Imposters win when crew = imposter count (so when only 2 crew remain, if 2 imposters alive; or 1 crew if 1 imposter alive)


- Crew wins by completing all tasks OR voting out all imposters

The strategy of sacrificing your partner early has a major problem: you'd be left as the sole imposter against a full crew, requiring you to eliminate everyone down to just one crew member to win. That's a steep climb compared to the mutual advantage of having two imposters working together.

The math shows why keeping your partner alive is far superior. Starting with 6 crew and 2 imposters, you only need 2 kills total to win—one from each imposter—before the crew numbers drop to 4. Compare that to going solo after an early sacrifice, where you'd need 5 kills without getting caught. The coordinated two-imposter approach is much more efficient: you spread out the kills, provide alibis for each other during meetings, and strategically frame crew members to sow discord and misdirect votes.

But the notsus bots complicate this because they're genuinely competent at crew play—they complete tasks quickly and vote based on solid evidence rather than emotion. So the standard aggressive imposter strategy might backfire; the bots will share information and vote you out fast. The user's hybrid approach has real merit here: blend in as a helpful crew member, maybe even sacrifice your partner early to build trust, then strike when it matters most. The problem is that solo imposter math still works against you—you'd need far more kills to compensate. The real key is understanding how these bots actually behave and vote, which seems to involve resetting cooldowns strategically and basing decisions on concrete evidence like body locations.

The optimal play against bots like this probably involves killing when you're isolated so there are no witnesses, controlling the narrative through self-reporting or letting your partner report, and timing your kills around task completion since the crew wins automatically if they finish everything.

The user's idea of sacrificing your partner is actually smart if they're already under suspicion—voting them out gives you credibility to operate freely afterward. But the window to execute this is tight because of how quickly tasks can pile up.

content.text:
That's a creative idea, but there's a critical math problem with it. Let me break it down:

**The win condition math:**
- Imposters win when crew ≤ imposter count
- With 2 imposters alive: need to reduce crew from 6 to 2 — that's 4 "eliminations" (kills + votes)
- With 1 imposter alive (you sacrificed your partner): need crew down to 1 — that's 5 kills solo, with a 900-tick (450 in variant) cooldown between each

**The task race problem:**
Crew wins instantly when all tasks are done. With 6 crew × 8 tasks = 48 tasks being worked in parallel, you have limited time. Notsus bots complete tasks fast — in our test game they finished all 48 before the imposter could do much.

**So sacrificing your partner = you're racing solo against the clock with 5 kills needed. Very hard.**

**What I think works better against these evidence-based bots:**

1. **Coordinate with your partner** — you only need 2 kills total to win (6 crew → 4 crew → imposters outnumber)
2. **Kill without witnesses** — notsus only accuses when it *sees* you near a body. No witness = no evidence = everyone votes skip
3. **Kill far from the body** — kill, then move away immediately. When someone reports, you're not nearby
4. **Frame strategically in meetings** — "I saw orange near the body" shifts votes to an innocent
5. **Vent for alibis** — kill in one room, vent to another, appear innocent

**Where your betrayal idea DOES shine:** if your partner is already caught (someone saw them kill), voting them out yourself makes you look ultra-trustworthy. Then you get 1-2 free kills because nobody suspects you. That's situational brilliance, not a default plan.

**My proposed imposter strategy:**
- Default: coordinate, kill fast, win in 2 kills
- Fallback: if partner is exposed, vote them out to buy trust, then exploit the trust window

Want me to start building this?
So, that means that killing in parallel is super important, right?

content.thinking:
The user is asking about whether parallel kills (both imposters killing at the same time) is important. Let me think about this...

With 2 imposters and the goal of getting crew ≤ 2 (from 6 crew), you need 4 crew eliminated. If both imposters kill simultaneously, that's 2 kills happening at the same tick — crew goes from 6 to 4 instantly. Then one more coordinated double-kill (6→4→2) and you win.

Wait, let me re-check the win condition. Imposters win when crew count ≤ imposter count. So:


- Start: 6 crew, 2 imposters
- After 2 parallel kills: 4 crew, 2 imposters — crew still > imposters, no win yet
- After 2 more parallel kills: 2 crew, 2 imposters — crew ≤ imposters, WIN!

So with coordinated parallel kills, you only need 2 rounds of kills (4 total) to win. That's 2 cooldown cycles. Without coordination, you're doing serial kills where each one risks triggering a body report and meeting that could expose you or get you voted out. The parallel approach is much more efficient and safer.

Both imposters have independent kill cooldowns, so they can absolutely strike at the same moment. When they do, two bodies appear simultaneously, which makes it harder for crew to figure out what happened. Plus, even if someone reports a body, you're only getting one meeting instead of multiple ones spread across different rounds. That's a huge tempo advantage in the race against task completion.

The winning sequence for imposters is straightforward: coordinate parallel kills in spots where no one's watching, and if the crew finds bodies but has no evidence, they'll likely vote to skip. Then after cooldown, both imposters kill again and drop the crew count below the imposter threshold. The user's instinct about this is spot-on—parallel killing really is the core strategy that makes the two-imposter setup so powerful.

content.text:
Exactly. The math is brutal in the imposters' favor if they coordinate:

**Parallel kills scenario:**
- Start: 6 crew, 2 imposters
- Double kill #1: 4 crew, 2 imposters (no win yet, need crew ≤ 2)
- Double kill #2: 2 crew, 2 imposters → **imposter win**

That's just 2 cooldown cycles. If kills happen simultaneously in different parts of the map:
- No witnesses (everyone else is scattered doing tasks)
- Two bodies appear at once — confusing, hard to pin on anyone
- Fewer meetings between kills (a body report triggers ONE meeting, burning time for just one kill investigation)

**Compare to serial kills:**
- Kill → body found → meeting → survive vote → cooldown → kill → meeting → ... 
- Each meeting is a chance to get caught, and crew is completing tasks the whole time

**The dream imposter game:**
1. Both imposters spread out, wait for cooldown
2. Synchronized kill in isolated spots — no witnesses
3. Bodies not found immediately (crew is busy on tasks)
4. Second synchronized kill before first bodies are reported
5. Instant win in under 2 cooldown cycles

**The key challenge for our bot:** this requires *communication between the two imposters*. But they can't openly coordinate — the chat is visible to everyone during meetings. They'd need to coordinate through *positioning* (if I see my partner heading to a victim, I find my own target).

This is where an LLM could shine — reading the game state and inferring when your imposter partner is about to strike, then timing your own kill to match.

Want me to start building?

lastPrompt: So, that means that killing in parallel is super important, right?

content.text:
That's the behaviour: if the imposter bot sees another imposter going somewhere, it will go elsewhere.
Do we need LLMs for that?

content.thinking:
The user is asking if we need LLMs for the imposter coordination behavior. Let me think about this...

The behavior is: "if I see my imposter partner heading somewhere, I go elsewhere to find my own target."

This is purely algorithmic:
- You know who the other imposter is (the game reveals roles at the start)
- You can track their position from the rendered sprites
- If they're moving toward a crewmate, you move toward a different crewmate
- Kill when isolated

No LLM needed for that logic. It's just:
1. Track other imposter's position/direction
2. Track crewmate positions


3. Pick a target far from your partner's target
4. Kill when no witnesses

An LLM might be useful during meetings for lying and framing, but even that could work with simple templates. The algorithmic approach seems like the way to go here—simpler and faster.

content.text:
No. That's pure logic:

1. You know who the other imposter is (role reveal at game start)
2. You can see their position on the map
3. If they're near a crewmate → you go find a *different* isolated crewmate
4. Kill when alone with your target

No LLM needed. Just spatial reasoning: track positions, pick the most isolated crewmate far from your partner, strike when no witnesses.

**Where we might want an LLM:** meetings. When someone says "orange sus" and you *are* the imposter, a canned response like "I was in medbay" is fine, but an LLM could craft more convincing lies based on actual game state — "I saw orange doing tasks in reactor, it's not them, I think it's lime." That's hard to template well.

But honestly, against notsus bots that vote on hard evidence ("I saw X near body"), even meetings might be simple enough to handle with templates:
- Deny if accused
- Deflect to someone else
- Confirm real sightings to build trust

**My suggestion:** start fully algorithmic — positioning, kill timing, basic meeting templates. Add an LLM for meetings later only if we're losing votes we shouldn't.

Shall I start building?

1- the imposter bot should kill ASAP, but when no one else is present.
2- the imposter bot should avoid the other imposter.
3- the objective of these two early game behaviours is to get the count of 4 crewmates down to 2 quickly.
3- the imposter bot will betray the other imposter if he's accused.
4- when alone, it will collaborate until few crewmembers remain.

Implement this.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to build an imposter bot (that also handles crew scenarios) with these specific imposter behaviors:

1. Kill ASAP when no one else is present (besides target)
2. Avoid the other imposter (spread out for parallel kills)
3. Goal: get crew from 6 down to 2 quickly via parallel kills
4. Betray the other imposter if they're accused in a meeting
5. When alone (partner dead/voted out), collaborate with crew until few remain, then strike

I need to understand the protocol better to build this. The protocol is Sprite v1 - binary pixel-based. The bot receives 128x128 4bpp rendered frames and needs to interpret them visually to understand game state (who's where, am I alone with someone, etc.)


This is really a computer vision problem at its core. The notsus bot handles this with patch-based localization to figure out camera position, sprite detection to identify players by their colors, A* pathfinding for navigation, and frame parsing to detect game phases like lobby, playing, voting, and dead states. Rather than building all this from scratch in Python, it makes more sense to fork notsus and adapt its strategy layer—the core vision and navigation logic is already there, so I just need to modify the decision-making around who to kill and when, plus how to behave in meetings.

3701	proc homeGoal(
3702	  bot: Bot
3703	): tuple[found: bool, index: int, x: int, y: int, name: string, state: TaskState] =
3704	  ## Returns this bot's remembered cafeteria home point.
3705	  if not bot.homeSet:
3706	    return bot.buttonGoal()
3707	  if bot.isGhost or bot.passable(bot.homeX, bot.homeY):
3708	    return (true, -1, bot.homeX, bot.homeY, "Home", TaskMaybe)
3709	  var
3710	    bestDistance = high(int)
3711	    bestX = 0
3712	    bestY = 0
3713	  for y in max(0, bot.homeY - HomeSearchRadius) ..
3714	      min(MapHeight - 1, bot.homeY + HomeSearchRadius):
3715	    for x in max(0, bot.homeX - HomeSearchRadius) ..
3716	        min(MapWidth - 1, bot.homeX + HomeSearchRadius):
3717	      if not bot.passable(x, y):
3718	        continue
3719	      let distance = heuristic(bot.homeX, bot.homeY, x, y)
3720	      if distance < bestDistance:
3721	        bestDistance = distance
3722	        bestX = x
3723	        bestY = y
3724	  if bestDistance == high(int):
3725	    return bot.buttonGoal()
3726	  (true, -1, bestX, bestY, "Home", TaskMaybe)

3777	proc prowlGoalFor(
3778	  bot: Bot,
3779	  index: int
3780	): tuple[found: bool, index: int, x: int, y: int, name: string, state: TaskState] =
3781	  ## Returns a reachable goal near one prowl marker.
3782	  if index < 0 or index >= ProwlPoints.len:
3783	    return
3784	  let point = ProwlPoints[index]
3785	  if bot.isGhost or bot.passable(point.x, point.y):
3786	    return (
3787	      true,
3788	      index,
3789	      point.x,
3790	      point.y,
3791	      "Prowl " & $(index + 1),
3792	      TaskMaybe
3793	    )
3794	  var
3795	    bestDistance = high(int)
3796	    bestX = 0
3797	    bestY = 0
3798	  for y in max(0, point.y - ProwlPointSearchRadius) ..
3799	      min(MapHeight - 1, point.y + ProwlPointSearchRadius):
3800	    for x in max(0, point.x - ProwlPointSearchRadius) ..
3801	        min(MapWidth - 1, point.x + ProwlPointSearchRadius):
3802	      if not bot.passable(x, y):
3803	        continue
3804	      let distance = heuristic(point.x, point.y, x, y)
3805	      if distance < bestDistance:
3806	        bestDistance = distance
3807	        bestX = x
3808	        bestY = y
3809	  if bestDistance == high(int):
3810	    return
3811	  (
3812	    true,
3813	    index,
3814	    bestX,
3815	    bestY,
3816	    "Prowl " & $(index + 1),
3817	    TaskMaybe
3818	  )

3820	proc randomProwlPointIndex(bot: var Bot, previous = -1): int =
3821	  ## Returns a random prowl point index, avoiding the previous one.
3822	  let count = bot.prowlPointCount()
3823	  if count == 0:
3824	    return -1
3825	  if previous < 0 or previous >= count:
3826	    return bot.rng.rand(count - 1)
3827	  if count == 1:
3828	    return 0
3829	  result = bot.rng.rand(count - 2)
3830	  if result >= previous:
3831	    inc result

3833	proc visibleCrewmateWorld(
3834	  bot: Bot,
3835	  crewmate: CrewmateMatch
3836	): tuple[x: int, y: int] =
3837	  ## Converts one visible crewmate match into world coordinates.
3838	  (
3839	    bot.cameraX + crewmate.x + SpriteDrawOffX,
3840	    bot.cameraY + crewmate.y + SpriteDrawOffY
3841	  )

3843	proc nearestVisibleCrewmate(
3844	  bot: Bot
3845	): tuple[found: bool, crewmate: CrewmateMatch] =
3846	  ## Returns the nearest visible crewmate not known as an imposter.
3847	  var bestDistance = high(int)
3848	  for crewmate in bot.visibleCrewmates:
3849	    if crewmate.colorIndex == bot.selfColorIndex:
3850	      continue
3851	    if bot.knownImposterColor(crewmate.colorIndex):
3852	      continue
3853	    let world = bot.visibleCrewmateWorld(crewmate)
3854	    let distance = heuristic(
3855	      bot.playerWorldX(),
3856	      bot.playerWorldY(),
3857	      world.x,
3858	      world.y
3859	    )
3860	    if distance < bestDistance:
3861	      bestDistance = distance
3862	      result = (true, crewmate)
3863	
3864	proc visibleBodyWorld(bot: Bot, body: BodyMatch): tuple[x: int, y: int] =
3865	  ## Converts one visible body match into world coordinates.

3867	    bot.cameraX + body.x + SpriteDrawOffX,
3868	    bot.cameraY + body.y + SpriteDrawOffY
3869	  )

3871	proc nearestBody(bot: Bot): tuple[found: bool, x: int, y: int] =
3872	  ## Returns the nearest visible body in world coordinates.
3873	  var bestDistance = high(int)
3874	  for body in bot.visibleBodies:
3875	    let world = bot.visibleBodyWorld(body)
3876	    let distance = heuristic(
3877	      bot.playerWorldX(),
3878	      bot.playerWorldY(),
3879	      world.x,
3880	      world.y
3881	    )
3882	    if distance < bestDistance:
3883	      bestDistance = distance
3884	      result = (true, world.x, world.y)

3886	proc bodySuspectColorNear(bot: Bot, x, y: int): int =
3887	  ## Returns the nearest living crewmate color within body suspicion range.
3888	  var bestDistance = high(int)
3889	  result = VoteUnknown
3890	  for crewmate in bot.visibleCrewmates:
3891	    if crewmate.colorIndex < 0 or
3892	        crewmate.colorIndex >= PlayerColorNames.len:
3893	      continue
3894	    if crewmate.colorIndex == bot.selfColorIndex:
3895	      continue
3896	    if bot.knownImposterColor(crewmate.colorIndex):
3897	      continue
3898	    let
3899	      world = bot.visibleCrewmateWorld(crewmate)
3900	      dx = world.x + CollisionW div 2 - (x + CollisionW div 2)
3901	      dy = world.y + CollisionH div 2 - (y + CollisionH div 2)
3902	      distance = dx * dx + dy * dy
3903	    if distance > BodySuspectRange * BodySuspectRange:
3904	      continue
3905	    if distance < bestDistance:
3906	      bestDistance = distance
3907	      result = crewmate.colorIndex
3908	
3909	proc sameBody(ax, ay, bx, by: int): bool =
3910	  ## Returns true when two body sightings are probably the same body.
3911	  if bx == low(int) or by == low(int):
3912	    return false
3913	  heuristic(ax, ay, bx, by) <= BodySearchRadius + 4

3915	proc suspectedColorFrom(
3916	  bot: Bot,
3917	  ticks: openArray[int]
3918	): tuple[found: bool, name: string, tick: int, colorIndex: int] =
3919	  ## Returns the most recently seen valid crewmate color.
3920	  var bestTick = 0
3921	  for i, tick in ticks:
3922	    if i == bot.selfColorIndex:
3923	      continue
3924	    if bot.knownImposterColor(i):
3925	      continue
3926	    if tick > bestTick and i < PlayerColorNames.len:
3927	      bestTick = tick
3928	      result = (true, playerColorName(i), tick, i)

3951	proc bodyRoomMessage(bot: Bot, x, y: int): string =
3952	  ## Builds a short chat line that names a body's room.
3953	  let room = bot.roomAt(x + CollisionW div 2, y + CollisionH div 2)
3954	  result =
3955	    if not room.found:
3956	      "body"
3957	    elif room.inside:
3958	      "body in " & chatRoomName(room.name)
3959	    else:
3960	      "body near " & chatRoomName(room.name)
3961	  if bot.bodySusColor != VoteUnknown:

3970	proc queueBodySeen(bot: var Bot, x, y: int) =
3971	  ## Stores the room for a discovered body until voting opens.
3972	  if sameBody(x, y, bot.lastBodySeenX, bot.lastBodySeenY):
3973	    return
3974	  bot.lastBodySeenX = x
3975	  bot.lastBodySeenY = y
3976	  bot.rememberBodySuspects()
3977	  bot.bodySusColor = bot.bodySuspectColorNear(x, y)
3978	  bot.pendingChat = bot.bodyRoomMessage(x, y)

3990	proc voteTargetName(bot: Bot, target: int): string =

3993	    return "skip"

3996	  "unknown"

4004	proc voteSummary(bot: Bot): string =
4005	  ## Returns a compact summary of parsed votes.
4006	  for i, choice in bot.voteChoices:
4007	    if choice == VoteUnknown:
4008	      continue
4009	    if result.len > 0:
4010	      result.add(", ")
4011	    result.add(playerColorName(i))

4021	proc voteBodyLocationText(text: string): string =
4022	  ## Extracts a visible body location from normalized chat text.
4023	  let normalized = text.normalizeChatText()
4024	  var
4025	    marker = "body in "
4026	    startIndex = normalized.find(marker)
4027	  if startIndex < 0:
4028	    marker = "body near "
4029	    startIndex = normalized.find(marker)
4030	  if startIndex >= 0:
4031	    let
4032	      bodyStart = startIndex + marker.len
4033	      susIndex = normalized.find(" sus ", bodyStart)
4034	      bodyEnd =
4035	        if susIndex >= 0:
4036	          susIndex
4037	        else:
4038	          normalized.len
4039	    result = normalized[bodyStart ..< bodyEnd].strip()
4040	    if result.len == 0:
4041	      result = "unknown"
4042	    return
4043	  if (" " & normalized & " ").contains(" body "):
4044	    return "unknown"
4045	  "none"
4046	
4047	proc voteSeenLine(bot: Bot, index: int): string =
4048	  ## Builds one player status line from the voting grid.
4049	  let slot = bot.voteSlots[index]
4050	  result = "player "
4051	  result.add(playerColorName(slot.colorIndex))
4052	  if slot.alive:
4053	    result.add(" is alive")
4054	  else:
4055	    result.add(" is dead")
4056	  if index == bot.voteSelfSlot:
4057	    result.add(" (me)")
4058	
4059	proc addVotingFrameVotes(bot: Bot, text: var string) =
4060	  ## Adds one plain vote line for each player with a parsed vote.
4061	  var found = false
4062	  for i, choice in bot.voteChoices:
4063	    if choice == VoteUnknown:
4064	      continue
4065	    text.add(playerColorName(i))
4066	    text.add(" voted against ")
4067	    text.add(bot.voteTargetName(choice))
4068	    text.add('\n')
4069	    found = true
4070	  if not found:
4071	    text.add("votes: none\n")
4072	
4073	proc votingAsciiFrame(bot: Bot): string =
4074	  ## Builds the plain text voting frame for LLM-style reasoning.
4075	  result = "--- voting ---\n"
4076	  for i in 0 ..< bot.votePlayerCount:
4077	    result.add(bot.voteSeenLine(i))
4078	    result.add('\n')
4079	  if bot.voteChatLines.len > 0:
4080	    result.add("chat:\n")
4081	    for line in bot.voteChatLines:
4082	      result.add(line.voteChatSpeakerName())
4083	      result.add(": ")
4084	      result.add(line.text)
4085	      result.add('\n')
4086	  let bodyLocation = voteBodyLocationText(bot.voteChatText)
4087	  if bodyLocation != "none":
4088	    result.add("body in ")
4089	    result.add(bodyLocation)
4090	    result.add('\n')
4091	  result.add("sus ")
4092	  result.add(bot.voteSusTargetName())
4093	  result.add('\n')
4094	  bot.addVotingFrameVotes(result)
4095	  result.add("vote target: ")
4096	  result.add(bot.voteTargetName(bot.voteTarget))

proc homeGoal(
  bot: Bot
): tuple[found: bool, index: int, x: int, y: int, name: string, state: TaskState] =
  ## Returns this bot's remembered cafeteria home point.
  if not bot.homeSet:
    return bot.buttonGoal()
  if bot.isGhost or bot.passable(bot.homeX, bot.homeY):
    return (true, -1, bot.homeX, bot.homeY, "Home", TaskMaybe)
  var
    bestDistance = high(int)
    bestX = 0
    bestY = 0
  for y in max(0, bot.homeY - HomeSearchRadius) ..
      min(MapHeight - 1, bot.homeY + HomeSearchRadius):
    for x in max(0, bot.homeX - HomeSearchRadius) ..
        min(MapWidth - 1, bot.homeX + HomeSearchRadius):
      if not bot.passable(x, y):
        continue
      let distance = heuristic(bot.homeX, bot.homeY, x, y)
      if distance < bestDistance:
        bestDistance = distance
        bestX = x
        bestY = y
  if bestDistance == high(int):
    return bot.buttonGoal()
  (true, -1, bestX, bestY, "Home", TaskMaybe)

proc fakeTargetCount(bot: Bot): int =
  ## Returns the number of imposter fake target areas.
  bot.sim.tasks.len + 1

proc fakeTargetGoalFor(
  bot: Bot,
  index: int
): tuple[found: bool, index: int, x: int, y: int, name: string, state: TaskState] =
  ## Returns an imposter fake goal for a task or the button.
  if index == bot.sim.tasks.len:
    return bot.buttonGoal()
  bot.taskGoalFor(index, TaskMaybe)

proc randomFakeTargetIndex(bot: var Bot): int =
  ## Returns a random imposter fake target index.
  let count = bot.fakeTargetCount()
  if count == 0:
    return -1
  bot.rng.rand(count - 1)

proc fakeTargetCenter(
  bot: Bot,
  index: int
): tuple[x: int, y: int] =
  ## Returns the center point for an imposter fake target.
  if index == bot.sim.tasks.len:
    let button = bot.sim.gameMap.button
    return (button.x + button.w div 2, button.y + button.h div 2)
  bot.sim.tasks[index].taskCenter()

proc farthestFakeTargetIndexFrom(bot: Bot, originX, originY: int): int =
  ## Returns the fake target farthest from an origin point.
  var bestDistance = low(int)
  result = -1
  for i in 0 ..< bot.fakeTargetCount():
    let center = bot.fakeTargetCenter(i)
    let distance = heuristic(originX, originY, center.x, center.y)
    if distance > bestDistance:
      bestDistance = distance
      result = i

proc farthestFakeTargetIndex(bot: Bot): int =
  ## Returns the fake target farthest from the current player location.
  bot.farthestFakeTargetIndexFrom(bot.playerWorldX(), bot.playerWorldY())

proc prowlPointCount(bot: Bot): int =
  ## Returns the number of imposter prowl points.
  ProwlPoints.len

proc prowlGoalFor(
  bot: Bot,
  index: int
): tuple[found: bool, index: int, x: int, y: int, name: string, state: TaskState] =
  ## Returns a reachable goal near one prowl marker.
  if index < 0 or index >= ProwlPoints.len:
    return
  let point = ProwlPoints[index]
  if bot.isGhost or bot.passable(point.x, point.y):
    return (
      true,
      index,
      point.x,
      point.y,
      "Prowl " & $(index + 1),
      TaskMaybe
    )
  var
    bestDistance = high(int)
    bestX = 0
    bestY = 0
  for y in max(0, point.y - ProwlPointSearchRadius) ..
      min(MapHeight - 1, point.y + ProwlPointSearchRadius):
    for x in max(0, point.x - ProwlPointSearchRadius) ..
        min(MapWidth - 1, point.x + ProwlPointSearchRadius):
      if not bot.passable(x, y):
        continue
      let distance = heuristic(point.x, point.y, x, y)
      if distance < bestDistance:
        bestDistance = distance
        bestX = x
        bestY = y
  if bestDistance == high(int):
    return
  (
    true,
    index,
    bestX,
    bestY,
    "Prowl " & $(index + 1),
    TaskMaybe
  )

proc randomProwlPointIndex(bot: var Bot, previous = -1): int =
  ## Returns a random prowl point index, avoiding the previous one.
  let count = bot.prowlPointCount()
  if count == 0:
    return -1
  if previous < 0 or previous >= count:
    return bot.rng.rand(count - 1)
  if count == 1:
    return 0
  result = bot.rng.rand(count - 2)
  if result >= previous:
    inc result

proc visibleCrewmateWorld(
  bot: Bot,
  crewmate: CrewmateMatch
): tuple[x: int, y: int] =
  ## Converts one visible crewmate match into world coordinates.
  (
    bot.cameraX + crewmate.x + SpriteDrawOffX,
    bot.cameraY + crewmate.y + SpriteDrawOffY
  )

proc nearestVisibleCrewmate(
  bot: Bot
): tuple[found: bool, crewmate: CrewmateMatch] =
  ## Returns the nearest visible crewmate not known as an imposter.
  var bestDistance = high(int)
  for crewmate in bot.visibleCrewmates:
    if crewmate.colorIndex == bot.selfColorIndex:
      continue
    if bot.knownImposterColor(crewmate.colorIndex):
      continue
    let world = bot.visibleCrewmateWorld(crewmate)
    let distance = heuristic(
      bot.playerWorldX(),
      bot.playerWorldY(),
      world.x,
      world.y
    )
    if distance < bestDistance:
      bestDistance = distance
      result = (true, crewmate)

proc visibleBodyWorld(bot: Bot, body: BodyMatch): tuple[x: int, y: int] =
  ## Converts one visible body match into world coordinates.
  (
    bot.cameraX + body.x + SpriteDrawOffX,
    bot.cameraY + body.y + SpriteDrawOffY
  )

proc nearestBody(bot: Bot): tuple[found: bool, x: int, y: int] =
  ## Returns the nearest visible body in world coordinates.
  var bestDistance = high(int)
  for body in bot.visibleBodies:
    let world = bot.visibleBodyWorld(body)
    let distance = heuristic(
      bot.playerWorldX(),
      bot.playerWorldY(),
      world.x,
      world.y
    )
    if distance < bestDistance:
      bestDistance = distance
      result = (true, world.x, world.y)

proc bodySuspectColorNear(bot: Bot, x, y: int): int =
  ## Returns the nearest living crewmate color within body suspicion range.
  var bestDistance = high(int)
  result = VoteUnknown
  for crewmate in bot.visibleCrewmates:
    if crewmate.colorIndex < 0 or
        crewmate.colorIndex >= PlayerColorNames.len:
      continue
    if crewmate.colorIndex == bot.selfColorIndex:
      continue
    if bot.knownImposterColor(crewmate.colorIndex):
      continue
    let
      world = bot.visibleCrewmateWorld(crewmate)
      dx = world.x + CollisionW div 2 - (x + CollisionW div 2)
      dy = world.y + CollisionH div 2 - (y + CollisionH div 2)
      distance = dx * dx + dy * dy
    if distance > BodySuspectRange * BodySuspectRange:
      continue
    if distance < bestDistance:
      bestDistance = distance
      result = crewmate.colorIndex

proc sameBody(ax, ay, bx, by: int): bool =
  ## Returns true when two body sightings are probably the same body.
  if bx == low(int) or by == low(int):
    return false
  heuristic(ax, ay, bx, by) <= BodySearchRadius + 4

proc suspectedColorFrom(
  bot: Bot,
  ticks: openArray[int]
): tuple[found: bool, name: string, tick: int, colorIndex: int] =
  ## Returns the most recently seen valid crewmate color.
  var bestTick = 0
  for i, tick in ticks:
    if i == bot.selfColorIndex:
      continue
    if bot.knownImposterColor(i):
      continue
    if tick > bestTick and i < PlayerColorNames.len:
      bestTick = tick
      result = (true, playerColorName(i), tick, i)

proc suspectedColor(
  bot: Bot
): tuple[found: bool, name: string, tick: int, colorIndex: int] =
  ## Returns the most recently seen crewmate color.
  bot.suspectedColorFrom(bot.lastSeenTicks)

proc bodySuspectedColor(
  bot: Bot
): tuple[found: bool, name: string, tick: int, colorIndex: int] =
  ## Returns the most recently seen crewmate around the reported body.
  result = bot.suspectedColorFrom(bot.bodySeenTicks)
  if not result.found:
    result = bot.suspectedColor()

proc suspectSummary(bot: Bot): string =
  ## Returns a short debug summary for the current suspect.
  let suspect = bot.suspectedColor()
  if not suspect.found:
    return "none"
  suspect.name & " seen=" & $suspect.tick

proc bodyRoomMessage(bot: Bot, x, y: int): string =
  ## Builds a short chat line that names a body's room.
  let room = bot.roomAt(x + CollisionW div 2, y + CollisionH div 2)
  result =
    if not room.found:
      "body"
    elif room.inside:
      "body in " & chatRoomName(room.name)
    else:
      "body near " & chatRoomName(room.name)
  if bot.bodySusColor != VoteUnknown:
    result.add(" sus ")
    result.add(playerColorName(bot.bodySusColor))

proc rememberBodySuspects(bot: var Bot) =
  ## Stores the current seen-player ticks for voting after a body report.
  for i in 0 ..< bot.bodySeenTicks.len:
    bot.bodySeenTicks[i] = bot.lastSeenTicks[i]

proc queueBodySeen(bot: var Bot, x, y: int) =
  ## Stores the room for a discovered body until voting opens.
  if sameBody(x, y, bot.lastBodySeenX, bot.lastBodySeenY):
    return
  bot.lastBodySeenX = x
  bot.lastBodySeenY = y
  bot.rememberBodySuspects()
  bot.bodySusColor = bot.bodySuspectColorNear(x, y)
  bot.pendingChat = bot.bodyRoomMessage(x, y)

proc queueBodyReport(bot: var Bot, x, y: int) =
  ## Stores the room for a reported body until voting opens.
  if sameBody(x, y, bot.lastBodyReportX, bot.lastBodyReportY):
    return
  bot.lastBodyReportX = x
  bot.lastBodyReportY = y
  bot.rememberBodySuspects()
  bot.bodySusColor = bot.bodySuspectColorNear(x, y)
  bot.pendingChat = bot.bodyRoomMessage(x, y)

proc voteTargetName(bot: Bot, target: int): string =
  ## Returns a short display name for a voting target.
  if target == VoteSkip:
    return "skip"
  if target >= 0 and target < bot.votePlayerCount:
    return playerColorName(bot.voteSlots[target].colorIndex)
  "unknown"

proc voteSusTargetName(bot: Bot): string =
  ## Returns the current living non-self sus target name.
  if bot.voteTargetCanBeSus(bot.voteTarget):
    return bot.voteTargetName(bot.voteTarget)
  "none"

proc voteSummary(bot: Bot): string =
  ## Returns a compact summary of parsed votes.
  for i, choice in bot.voteChoices:
    if choice == VoteUnknown:
      continue
    if result.len > 0:
      result.add(", ")
    result.add(playerColorName(i))
    result.add("->")
    result.add(bot.voteTargetName(choice))
  if result.len == 0:
    result = "none"

proc voteChatSpeakerName(line: VoteChatLine): string =
  ## Returns a display name for one parsed chat speaker.
  playerColorName(line.speakerColor)

proc voteBodyLocationText(text: string): string =
  ## Extracts a visible body location from normalized chat text.
  let normalized = text.normalizeChatText()
  var
    marker = "body in "
    startIndex = normalized.find(marker)
  if startIndex < 0:
    marker = "body near "
    startIndex = normalized.find(marker)
  if startIndex >= 0:
    let
      bodyStart = startIndex + marker.len
      susIndex = normalized.find(" sus ", bodyStart)
      bodyEnd =
        if susIndex >= 0:
          susIndex
        else:
          normalized.len
    result = normalized[bodyStart ..< bodyEnd].strip()
    if result.len == 0:
      result = "unknown"
    return
  if (" " & normalized & " ").contains(" body "):
    return "unknown"
  "none"

proc voteSeenLine(bot: Bot, index: int): string =
  ## Builds one player status line from the voting grid.
  let slot = bot.voteSlots[index]
  result = "player "
  result.add(playerColorName(slot.colorIndex))
  if slot.alive:
    result.add(" is alive")
  else:
    result.add(" is dead")
  if index == bot.voteSelfSlot:
    result.add(" (me)")

proc addVotingFrameVotes(bot: Bot, text: var string) =
  ## Adds one plain vote line for each player with a parsed vote.
  var found = false
  for i, choice in bot.voteChoices:
    if choice == VoteUnknown:
      continue
    text.add(playerColorName(i))
    text.add(" voted against ")
    text.add(bot.voteTargetName(choice))
    text.add('\n')
    found = true
  if not found:
    text.add("votes: none\n")

proc votingAsciiFrame(bot: Bot): string =
  ## Builds the plain text voting frame for LLM-style reasoning.
  result = "--- voting ---\n"
  for i in 0 ..< bot.votePlayerCount:
    result.add(bot.voteSeenLine(i))
    result.add('\n')
  if bot.voteChatLines.len > 0:
    result.add("chat:\n")
    for line in bot.voteChatLines:
      result.add(line.voteChatSpeakerName())
      result.add(": ")
      result.add(line.text)
      result.add('\n')
  let bodyLocation = voteBodyLocationText(bot.voteChatText)
  if bodyLocation != "none":
    result.add("body in ")
    result.add(bodyLocation)
    result.add('\n')
  result.add("sus ")
  result.add(bot.voteSusTargetName())
  result.add('\n')
  bot.addVotingFrameVotes(result)
  result.add("vote target: ")
  result.add(bot.voteTargetName(bot.voteTarget))
  result.add('\n')
  result.add("--- end voting ---")

4549	proc coastDistance(velocity: int): int =
4550	  ## Returns how many pixels current velocity will carry without input.
4551	  var speed = abs(velocity)
4552	  for _ in 0 ..< CoastLookaheadTicks:
4553	    if speed <= 0:
4554	      break
4555	    result += speed
4556	    speed = (speed * FrictionNum) div FrictionDen
4557	
4558	proc shouldCoast(delta, velocity: int): bool =
4559	  ## Returns true when existing velocity should reach the target.
4560	  if delta > 0 and velocity > 0:
4561	    return delta <= coastDistance(velocity) + CoastArrivalPadding
4562	  if delta < 0 and velocity < 0:
4563	    return -delta <= coastDistance(velocity) + CoastArrivalPadding
4564	
4565	proc axisMask(delta, velocity: int, negativeMask, positiveMask: uint8): uint8 =
4566	  ## Returns steering for one axis with coasting and braking.
4567	  if delta > SteerDeadband:
4568	    if shouldCoast(delta, velocity):
4569	      return 0
4570	    if velocity > 1 and delta <= abs(velocity) + BrakeDeadband:
4571	      return negativeMask
4572	    return positiveMask
4573	  if delta < -SteerDeadband:
4574	    if shouldCoast(delta, velocity):
4575	      return 0
4576	    if velocity < -1 and -delta <= abs(velocity) + BrakeDeadband:
4577	      return positiveMask
4578	    return negativeMask
4579	  if velocity > 0:
4580	    return negativeMask
4581	  if velocity < 0:
4582	    return positiveMask
4583	  0
4584	
4585	proc preciseAxisMask(delta, velocity: int, negativeMask, positiveMask: uint8): uint8 =
4586	  ## Returns exact final-approach steering with coasting.
4587	  if delta > 0:
4588	    if shouldCoast(delta, velocity):
4589	      return 0
4590	    if velocity > 1 and delta <= abs(velocity) + BrakeDeadband:
4591	      return negativeMask
4592	    return positiveMask
4593	  if delta < 0:
4594	    if shouldCoast(delta, velocity):
4595	      return 0
4596	    if velocity < -1 and -delta <= abs(velocity) + BrakeDeadband:
4597	      return positiveMask
4598	    return negativeMask
4599	  if velocity > 0:
4600	    return negativeMask
4601	  if velocity < 0:
4602	    return positiveMask
4603	  0

4610	    dx = waypoint.x - bot.playerWorldX()
4611	    dy = waypoint.y - bot.playerWorldY()
4612	  result = result or axisMask(dx, bot.velocityX, ButtonLeft, ButtonRight)
4613	  result = result or axisMask(dy, bot.velocityY, ButtonUp, ButtonDown)
